query: anatomical origins of anterior portion of tongue

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue is principally involved in digestion, taste perception, and speech but also plays an important role in breathing. [1] It is attached via the extrinsic muscles to the hyoid bone, mandible, styloid process, soft palate, and pharynx and divided into two parts by the V-shaped sulcus terminalis. These two parts, an anterior two-thirds and a posterior one-third, are structurally and developmentally distinct. The foramen cecum at the apex of the sulcus terminalis indicates the site of embryonic origin of the thyroglossal duct. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The topography of the tongue encompasses the tip, lateral surfaces, ventral surface, dorsal surface, and base. The tip represents the most anterior part. The lateral edges separate the ventral surface from the dorsal surface. The ventral surface is the underside of the tongue, which opposes the floor of the mouth, while the dorsal surface is the upper surface. The base of the tongue comprises the posterior one-third, including tissue located behind the circumvallate papillae and extending to the vallecula, which is situated between the base of the tongue and the epiglottis. This region is embryologically distinct from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue can also be segmented into thirds. The tip constitutes the anterior one-third, while the posterior one-third refers to the tongue base. The middle third lies between the tip and the base. The anterior two-thirds is part of the oral cavity, whereas the posterior one-third, known as the tongue base, belongs to the oropharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The fibers of the styloglossus muscle can be divided into anterior and posterior bundles. Anteriorly, the bundles run bilaterally and meet at the midline of the floor of the tongue, forming an arch. The posterior bundles of the styloglossus muscle split into approximately 10 smaller bundles of fibers that enter the tongue. These smaller bundles course between the inferior longitudinal intrinsic muscles of the tongue and the genioglossus muscle and ultimately insert into the lingual septum. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Neck Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Anterior Triangle
retrieved_docs.content: There are eight branches of the external carotid artery, though only the four most proximal are within the boundaries of the anterior triangle. The superior thyroid artery exits from the anterior aspect of the carotid artery and descends to supply the superior pole of the thyroid gland, the thyrohyoid and cricothyroid muscles, and the internal larynx via the superior laryngeal artery. The ascending pharyngeal artery arises from the posterior aspect of the external carotid, just superior to the superior thyroid artery. This artery dives deep to pierce the pretracheal fascia and supply pharyngeal constrictor muscles. The lingual artery originates from the anterior surface of the external carotid and immediately passes deep to the mylohyoid muscle to supply the muscles of the tongue and the sublingual salivary gland. The facial artery also branches off of the anterior surface of the external carotid just superior to the lingual artery. It then passes deep to the posterior belly of the digastric and stylohyoid muscles and continues its tortuous course superficial to the mylohyoid muscle before hooking over the inferior edge of the mandible just anterior to the masseter muscle. The facial artery supplies the submandibular salivary gland, the soft palate, and many more structures of the face via its numerous branches. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatoglossus Muscle (Glossopalatinus, Palatoglossal) -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The muscle has its origin from the inferior surface of the palatine aponeurosis. It descends along with the muscle of the contralateral side in an inferior, forward, and lateral direction anterior to the palatine tonsil and gets inserted into the posterolateral surface of the tongue. Some fibers of the muscle spread over the dorsal surface of the tongue while the other fibers pass deep into the substance of the tongue and intermingle with one of the intrinsic muscles of the tongue (transverses linguae).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The lateral lingual swellings increase in size, eventually merging and overlapping the tuberculum impar. The merger of these two swellings forms the anterior two-thirds of the tongue. The mucosa overlying this part of the tongue originates from the first arch; thus, the sensory innervation to this area is from the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3). Meanwhile, the second, third, and fourth portions of the pharyngeal arch, which make up the copula, develop into the posterior one-third of the tongue. The mucosa overlying this part of the tongue has sensory innervation from the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX), which is a sign that the third arch overlaps that of the second. The third arch derivatives typically are associated with glossopharyngeal sensory innervation.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Muscles and Divisions of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The tongue is a muscular organ located in the mouth, central to various functions such as mastication (chewing), deglutition (swallowing), gustation (taste), speech, and articulation. The tongue is a midline structure with a symmetrical muscle arrangement, innervation, and blood supply and is divided into 2 mirrored halves by an avascular midline raphe, which may also contain adipose tissue and lymphatics. [5] Histologically, the tongue's surface is lined with keratinized and nonkeratinized stratified squamous epithelium, along with specialized sensory mucosa for taste perception.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The area of the tongue posterior to the sulcus terminalis is known as the root or base (posterior one-third of the tongue). [5] The body (anterior two-thirds of the tongue) is the largest part of the tongue and extends from the sulcus terminalis to the frenulum. [5] The body is further subdivided into anterior and posterior. The anterior body is the area below the hard palate, and the posterior body is underneath the soft palate. [5] A collection of nodular lymphatic tissue lies towards the posterior one-third of the tongue and is known as the lingual tonsil. The tongue's functions mainly include taste, speech, and food manipulation in the oral cavity. It also plays an essential role in breathing. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Originating at the apex of the styloid process of the temporal bone, the styloglossus muscle inserts into the lateral aspect of the tongue, namely into the fibers of the intrinsic longitudinal muscles and between the two parts of the hyoglossus muscle. External and internal fibers of the styloglossus muscle also fuse with parts of the superior pharyngeal constrictor muscle and the palatoglossus muscle, respectively, with three bundles traveling anteriorly towards the apex of the tongue and an inferior longitudinal bundle joining the inferior fibers of the genioglossus muscle. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglossal duct cysts can develop when there is a remnant at the point of embryologic origin of the thyroid gland, the foramen cecum. The thyroid gland begins to develop on the floor of the embryologic pharynx at the point called the foramen cecum, which is located at the dorsum of the posterior tongue. The developing gland then relocates to its ultimate destination in the anterior neck by migrating down the thyroglossal duct anterior to the hyoid and thyroid cartilages before stopping anterolateral to the superior aspect of the trachea.

retrieved_docs.title: Tongue Laceration -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The hypoglossal nerve controls all 8 tongue muscles. Perfusion of the tongue is provided by the paired lingual arteries, which arise from the external carotid system, and venous drainage passes through the lingual veins into the internal jugular veins (see Image . Arteries and Veins of the Tongue). The lingual nerve carries somatic sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, and taste sensation from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is provided by the chorda tympani nerve, whose fibers join the lingual nerve in the infratemporal fossa and continue with them into the tongue itself (see Images . The Mouth Cavity and Chorda Tympani). Taste and somatic sensation from the posterior one-third of the tongue—the base of the tongue—are carried by the glossopharyngeal nerve. The chemoreceptors that provide taste sensation are housed within taste buds, distributed across the tongue's dorsum (see Image . Tongue Papillae and Taste Bud Distribution). For more information on tongue anatomy, please see Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue .

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Corticobulbar Tract -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Corticobulbar tract carries upper motor neuron input to motor nuclei of trigeminal, facial, glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory, and hypoglossal nerves. The motor component of trigeminal nerves supplies muscles of mastication. The facial nerve supplies the muscles of facial expression. Glossopharyngeal, vagus, accessory nerve innervates muscles of the pharynx, muscles of larynx, sternocleidomastoid, and trapezius muscle. Tongue muscles receive fibers from the hypoglossal nerve. The corticobulbar tract indirectly affects muscles supplied by third, fourth, and sixth cranial nerve through medial longitudinal fasciculus and paramedian part of the pontine formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Artery -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Of relevance, there is an anatomical triangular landmark where the lingual artery can be located with consistency. Lesser’s triangle, which is inside the submandibular triangle, contains the lingual artery as it courses toward the apex of the tongue from its origin of the external carotid artery. Lesser’s triangle is bound by the anterior belly of digastric muscle inferiorly, the posterior belly of the digastric muscle posteriorly, and the hypoglossal nerve superiorly. [5] The floor of Lesser’s triangle is the hyoglossus muscle, and the lingual artery is found deep to it. [6] The accessibility of the lingual artery in Lesser’s triangle makes it a perfect target for surgery. The lingual artery has a diameter of about 2 to 5 mm, which increases as it approaches the external carotid artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Cavity (Mouth)  -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The innervation of the tongue is complex. Motor innervation of all intrinsic and extrinsic muscles, except for the palatoglossus comes from the hypoglossal nerve. The palatoglossus receives innervation from the vagus nerve. The anterior and posterior portions of the tongue have different taste and sensory innervation due to their different embryological origins. The anterior two-thirds of the tongue receives taste sensation by a branch of the facial nerve called the chorda tympani nerve whereas the general sensation of the anterior two-third tongue is by the lingual nerve, which is a branch of the mandibular branch of trigeminal. The posterior one-third of the tongue gets both its sensory and taste sensations from the glossopharyngeal nerve. [1] The buccal nerve, also a branch of the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve innervates the cheek.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Tongue -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The muscles of the tongue predominantly derive from the myoblasts which originate in the occipital somites. They receive their innervations from the XII cranial nerve (hypoglossal nerve) except the palatoglossus muscle. The muscles of the tongue include extrinsic and intrinsic muscles. The extrinsic muscles are four in number (genioglossus, palatoglossus, styloglossus, and hyoglossus) and originate from the structures adjacent to the tongue. They allow the tongue to move in all directions. On the other hand, the four paired intrinsic muscles which include superior longitudinal, inferior longitudinal, verticalis, and transverses muscle have their origin as well as insertion inside the tongue. They are responsible for changing the shape of the tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy; partial or complete, indicating that removal of the thyroid gland—whether in part or in entirety—is performed within this anatomical region. This procedure is also known by its Arabic name استئصال الدرقية, which serves as the parent term for the English description of thyroidectomy; partial or complete. Understanding that the thyroid gland resides in the head and neck area and recognizing both the English and Arabic terminology helps clinicians accurately plan and communicate surgical interventions for thyroid disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576

Excision is also the method for “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” and it is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating a relationship between excisional techniques and nasal aesthetic surgery. Within the hierarchical structure of clinical actions, the group labeled “Clinical actions A‑G” is a child of Excision, making Excision the parent concept for these actions. Conversely, certain spinal operations are explicitly excluded from the Excision category: laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) at lumbar, sacral, or thoracic levels (one or two vertebral segments) are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
retrieved_docs.content: of neck anatomy in head‑and‑neck neoplasms. The Arabic phrase “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” also serves as a mapping source for both metastatic sublingual gland neoplasms and malignant skin cancer (“سرطان الجلد الخبيث”), illustrating how diverse primary tumors can give rise to secondary malignant lesions in specified sites. This classification framework emphasizes the importance of disease taxonomy and anatomical localization for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.

Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas is morphologically associated with الورم السرطاني (cancerous tumor) and is mapped to the broader term cancer of pancreas, indicating that this anatomic variant is a subset of pancreatic cancer. Metastatic involvement of lymph nodes in the head and neck region is captured by several entities: metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of the head and neck, metastatic adenocarcinoma to lymph node of the head and neck, and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck. All three are classified as secondary malignancies, each “mapped to” the secondary‑malignancy group and described by inverse‑is‑a relationships to الورم السرطاني (cancerous tumor) or, for the squamous cell type, to سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck). Morphologically, metastatic adenocarcinoma to a head‑and‑neck lymph node is associated with سرطانة غدية نقيلية (metastatic glandular carcinoma),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly linked to the larynx, indicating that examinations and assessments targeting these structures focus on the laryngeal anatomy. The relationship identified as “indirect_procedure_site_of” connects the larynx to the broader category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” meaning that the larynx can serve as an indirect site for non‑operative therapeutic interventions within the respiratory system. Within this category, the relationship “has child” designates the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway) as a subordinate or child procedure. Consequently, the placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway is classified under “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” and may involve the larynx indirectly. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in coordinating diagnostic evaluations of the larynx with therapeutic airway management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2551

This connection highlights that thyroid malignancies arise within the endocrine system and involve the anatomical region of the head and neck, which is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted patient care.

In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it also serves as a methodological category for certain plastic or reconstructive surgeries in the facial region. Conversely, several laminectomy techniques—specifically decompressive laminectomy of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. This exclusion applies to cervical, lumbar (except spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic laminectomies involving one or two vertebral segments, as well as cervical laminectomies extending over more than two vertebral segments. Thus, Excision functions as an umbrella term for diverse operative procedures ranging from endocrine gland removal and biliary duct exploration to cranial and urogenital tumor resections, while clearly distinguishing itself from certain spinal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.
